cataloging_subject.label: The Sowdone of Babylone
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The anonymous Middle English Charlemagne romance represented by the unique text in Princeton University Library MS Garrett 140 and encoded in CME00053. This work-level record does not collapse the medieval poem, its lost or unknown Anglo-Norman source version, Garrett's physical codex, Hausknecht's 1881 edition, the University of Michigan encoding, or the repository XML into a single object.

catalog_summary.author: Anonymous Middle English poet-adapter
catalog_summary.date: Late fourteenth or early fifteenth century; commonly dated around 1400

notes: A conventional scholarly title normalized to Hausknecht's spelling; the unique manuscript has no formal title page.
Modernized title used by the Database of Middle English Romance and Lupack's edition.

scope.description: Long title of the 1854 and 1881 printed editions

notes: No historical author has been securely identified. The poem adapts Anglo-Norman Charlemagne material, but its precise exemplar is unknown.

assertion.notes: The broad source traditions are supported, but the exact Anglo-Norman version or physical exemplar is not identified.

scope.description: Major setting and object of attack and conquest in the narrative
Realm and campaign geography associated with Charlemagne and his peers
Campaign geography of the Ferumbras tradition

form_statements.description: A 3,274-line verse romance, principally in four-line abab quatrains with some eight-line stanzas.

subject_statements.label: Anti-Muslim polemic and religious violence
notes: The catalog term describes the poem's hostile conventional representation of Muslims and coerced or violent conversion; it does not endorse that representation.

scope.description: Ferumbras's conversion and conflict with his father, Laban

summaries.value: An anonymous Middle English Charlemagne romance that combines the destruction-of-Rome and Fierabras traditions. After the Sultan Laban's forces attack Rome and carry off relics, Charlemagne's campaigns bring violent conflict, sieges, the capture and rescue of the Twelve Peers, and the conversion of Ferumbras and Floripas. The poem frames Christian victory and Saracen defeat through strongly polemical religious and chivalric conventions.

The work survives uniquely in Princeton MS Garrett 140, ff. 1r-41r. CME00053 follows Hausknecht's 1881 edition and contains 3,274 verse lines. Its two XML LG elements reflect an encoding split around a line added by a later hand at line 370/f. 5r, not two independent intellectual parts.

extent.description: One continuous 3,274-line verse romance. The XML serializes the poem in two LG elements of 369 and 2,905 lines because line 370 is assigned to a later hand; those encoding groups are not separate work divisions.

content_parts.label: The Sowdone of Babylone
content_parts.summary: The complete continuous romance from the opening appeal to God through the concluding prayer and colophon.
notes: The source edition's title page is encoded separately as DIV1[1] but is paratext, not an additional content part of the medieval work.

related_works.title: Lost or unidentified Anglo-Norman source version for The Sowdone of Babylone
notes: Do not equate this abstract source version with any one surviving codex. Egerton MS 3028 and Hannover Ms IV 578 preserve cognate Destruction de Rome and Fierabras versions, not the Middle English target text and not a confirmed physical exemplar.

related_works.title: La Destruction de Rome
notes: A principal source analogue for the first movement of the Middle English romance; surviving versions in Egerton MS 3028 and Hannover Ms IV 578 have zero target-text overlap.

related_works.title: Fierabras
notes: A principal source analogue for the second movement of the Middle English romance; Hannover Ms IV 578 was directly consulted by Hausknecht but judged not to be its physical original.

evidence.summary: Grounds the encoded title, editor and publication data, 3,274-line extent, two verse-group serialization, line-370 hand change, print-page scope, and complete encoded text.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan CME item CME00053.
evidence.summary: Confirms the public item identity, source edition, title, and online encoded text.

evidence.citation: Database of Middle English Romance, The Sultan of Babylon.
evidence.summary: Primary descriptive source for title variant, late-fourteenth-/early-fifteenth-century date, East Midlands composition, romance genre, verse form and stanza pattern, plot, motifs, IMEV identifier, witness, and editions.

evidence.citation: Database of Middle English Romance, Princeton University Library MS Garrett 140.
evidence.summary: Supports unique-witness status, circa-1450 East Midlands manuscript date and origin, ff. 1r-41r, 3,274 lines, and IMEV 950.

evidence.citation: Princeton University Library catalog record for Garrett MS 140.
evidence.summary: Authoritative current witness title, date, region, physical description, unique text extent, foliation, provenance, and shelfmark.

evidence.locator: title page; introduction pp. v-liv; poem pp. 1-94
evidence.summary: Grounds the long title, anonymous authorship, editorial responsibility, source traditions, approximate composition assessment, immediate edition, and first-printing history.

evidence.citation: The Romaunce of the Sowdone of Babylone (Roxburghe Club, 1854), complete scan.
evidence.summary: Establishes the 1854 first publication, long editorial title, and direct manuscript basis.

evidence.citation: Jordi Sanchez-Marti, 'The Sowdoun of Babyloyne: A Description of the Manuscript,' SELIM 12 (2003-2004): 181-189.
evidence.summary: Supports title spelling, anonymous status, unique manuscript and provenance, composition around 1400, and the qualified relationship to an unknown or lost Anglo-Norman source version.

evidence.citation: Middle English Texts Series, Alan Lupack, ed., Three Middle English Charlemagne Romances.
evidence.summary: Supports the modernized title, Charlemagne-romance classification, plot description, and later comparison edition.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Egerton MS 3028.
evidence.summary: Confirms the physical codex and its La Destruccion de Rome and Fierabras folio ranges as cognate source witnesses, not target-text witnesses.

evidence.citation: Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz Bibliothek digital collections, Ms IV 578.
evidence.summary: Confirms the complete Hannover analogue and its Destruction de Rome and Fierabras contents; Hausknecht's direct consultation is grounded in the 1881 edition.

open_questions.question: Can the poem's broad late-fourteenth-/early-fifteenth-century composition range be narrowed securely?
next_steps: Review current linguistic, historical, and source scholarship before replacing the broad 1375-1425 normalization with a narrower date.

open_questions.question: What exact lost or surviving Anglo-Norman version did the Middle English poet adapt?
next_steps: Keep the abstract lost/unknown source distinct from Egerton MS 3028 and Hannover Ms IV 578; consult recent stemmatic work on La Destruction de Rome and Fierabras.

open_questions.question: Should the identifier be displayed as IMEV 950 or the more granular 950.1 used in some scholarship?
next_steps: Retain the current Database of Middle English Romance value 950 in the main identifier and add 950.1 only if an authoritative index record establishes its scope.

notes: The unique copy's circa-1450 date and East Midlands language are witness-level facts; the work's composition is earlier and remains broadly dated.
The XML's manuscript-side milestones reproduce Hausknecht's manuscript pagination and should not be presented as current Princeton foliation.
Hannover Ms IV 578 and BL Egerton MS 3028 contain only source analogues and have zero target-text overlap.
Religious-conflict and anti-Muslim subject terms describe historically situated content and rhetoric, not modern endorsement.
Hausknecht is displayed as editor because CME00053 represents his edition, while the medieval work remains anonymously authored.
